camera and my coffee and everything ready to go.

can only imagine.

You know, we're hiring for a couple of roles.

And, you know, one of those like this one is really about a combination of applying editorial sensibilities, you know, thinking about content the way it is put together, of course, just in a structural way, stylistic way.
And then also what audiences it is targeted towards.
All of those are very common skills for any content role.
The unique part about this role is that it takes those skills and puts them in a new context.
And the new context is that you are not actually a writer and you're not going to be a writer here necessarily.
You will write.
You will apply your writing skills.
But your job is really to manage a writer that is a writer.

An automated or semi-automated, I should say, pipeline of AI-driven workflows that creates assignments, organizes information into a way that allows it to create briefs for those assignments, then outlines from those briefs, and then graphs of articles, and then final output.
Of course, as a part of that are human interventions.

So the points in the pipeline where you understand, hey, you know, it has gone out and done a job for me, and it has come back, and it's provided me with some information, and then your job is to parse that, understand what it brought back, how closely it hewed to the assignment that you gave it.
Say, hey, I want you to go do this for me.
comes back and says, hey, I did this.
You're like, you know what, pretty good, but here's how it could be better.
Those human interventions, of course, help the system get better over time, but they also are vital because we view what we're building as like an augmented human arrangement.
This is not us.
Even a replacement arrangement, because the really great thing about AI and LLMs is that they can organize information very efficiently, but they cannot really synthesize new information.

They can take information that existed previously and organize it in new ways, sometimes even novel ways, or at least novel to us, you know, maybe not novel to someone somewhere on the planet, but novel to maybe the individual using it.
But they cannot really create new information or synthesize new things, and that is where humans come in.
We're able to say, hey, you're almost there, or you're close, you're within the 80 percentile of what I wanted, but here are the very specific ways that I would like you to improve.
And that articulation is a big part of this job.
So as opposed to, say, a writing-forward position, where you would be creating content, writing content a lot, and you're doing most of this work here, right?
It just all happens here, and then it comes out on the page, and you're...
To articulate to yourself as you go, no, no, I need more of this, or no, we need less of this, or let's adjust the structure or texture or flow.
Those processes now happen externally.
So the process happens, and then you need to find a way to articulate those processes or the ways that they are going off the rails and say, hey, no, I actually need this, this, or this.
So I just wanted to provide that broader context for the position that it is not necessarily a content creation position in a standard sense.

Yeah, it is, but it isn't, you know, and I want to set those expectations because the big thing that we're looking for, in addition to the standard, like content chops, we would expect anybody that came here to just be able to produce on their own decent content because it's helpful to be able to do that.
To then know what good content looks like.
You need to know how to judge what the element is bringing back, so you have to experience on that, so, yeah.
Yeah, exactly.
That's why we're kind of looking.

In that realm for people that have some of that background, because speaking from personal experience, it's very valuable to just instantly know, oh, I see what it's thinking here or what it's doing, the process that it is going through.
So, okay, here's how we'll go.
We'll talk to it.

And, like, that is really the second big part of the role is that systems-level thinking about, hey, I'm willing to dig into the architecture of how this works to understand the way that the automation steps are laying this out, what tweaks I can provide or input I can provide, and, frankly, even a layer deeper, what feedback can I provide to the engineering team or to the leadership team or whatever to say, hey, you know, we've got a blocker here.
It's doing this over and over, this repetitive issue, or this is just not quite where it needs to be.
And I think, you know, my gut tells me this could be improved as a process rather than me having to just do it manually every time, right?
The goal, the end goal.

The goal is to be able to provide people with enough raw additional power to take care of the laborious, repetitive stuff, and then allow them that extra time to spend at the outline stage and the final draft stage where they can be the most impactful.
They outline, of course, understanding the real drive that the client needs or the drive towards the goal that the client is trying to achieve.
Hey, this won't achieve that or this is not necessary to meet the goal or whatever.
That's the outline.
And then, of course, the final draft is like, oh, there's some stylistic things that I just don't like.
A little bit of texture, a little bit of a deeper dive on this particular fact or fact-checking or whatever it is.
That extra percentage of time that you would normally be spent laboriously doing the research, going out to the web, finding research on the topic, pulling that information back.
Writing, rewriting, finding something else, and then going back.
Yeah.
Exactly.

I mean, I think there is, so I am, you know, I'm a writer, longtime writer, editor, and made a living for a very long time with my brain, you know, writing, hopefully, what I thought to be unique, unique thoughts and content.

I have come to appreciate that there is a very real augmentation of lift that allows you to, if you look at it through the right lens, it should allow you to exercise above and beyond your abilities and then use those abilities to make the difference.
And what I mean by that is like, I'll give you a prime example, like a pragmatic example.
When we were, so I ran TechCrunch for 10 years.
It's like a tech website about startups and technology.
And we were a relatively small team, you know, about 100 people or so total and about 65 or 70 editorial folks.
And we were competing with like the New York Times for stories on a regular basis.
And they have like 1500 journalists, right?
So like we definitely were punching above our pay grade in a lot of ways, getting good scoops, et cetera, as you do.
This is the job of the journalist, right?
Of the news hound.
But there were plenty of projects that we came across where...
We realized we just didn't have the person power to do what we wanted to do.
The Panama Papers is a prime example.
When they released a bunch of documentation about a bunch of political goings-on that happened to coincide with finance and tech and a bunch of things.
And it was essentially a bunch of data.
A lot of PDFs, a lot of databases, a big dump of information.
And we were, you know, we would love to dig in, especially where it crossed over our world.
You know, the tech world, there were definitely some VCs mentioned, some venture capitalists and some tech people.
You know, we wanted to kind of dive in and pull those out.
We did the best we could.

But if we had, at the time, had access to LLMs, especially localized ones, that we could feed the information and ask it to pattern match for us, to parse that information, pull out details that are, you know, related to each other, to give it patterns and have it disprove or prove those patterns.
We felt we're there, you know, things like that.
It would have enabled us to punch above our weight in a much better way, to compete with the newsrooms that had those hundreds of people that they could turn towards this task.
so much a small team can do compared to bigger.
